facts.label: Minimum approved tank size
facts.note: Georgia's homeowner guide says the minimum approved tank size is 1,000 gallons and ties that baseline to a 3- or 4-bedroom house.

facts.label: Public flow anchor
facts.note: Georgia's public homeowner material uses 150 gallons per bedroom per day for a single-family residence.

facts.label: Garbage disposal rule
facts.note: Georgia is unusually explicit here: the homeowner guide says homes with garbage disposals need a septic tank that is 50 percent larger.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: County environmental health office
facts.note: Georgia homeowners usually move from the estimate into county environmental health review, not a one-size statewide permit desk.

facts.label: Public sizing example
facts.note: Pennsylvania DEP's homeowner page uses 900 gallons or larger as the public example for a 3-bedroom home.

facts.label: Primary sizing basis
facts.value: Bedrooms
facts.sourceSection: How septic tank size is set
facts.note: DEP tells homeowners tank sizes are generally based on the number of bedrooms in the home.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Municipality or local agency and Sewage Enforcement Officer
facts.note: Pennsylvania is strong for local workflow content because DEP makes the municipality or local agency and the Sewage Enforcement Officer central to the actual process.

facts.label: Local SEO lookup
facts.value: County SEO directory
facts.sourceSection: Active Sewage Enforcement Officers by county
facts.note: The state publishes an active SEO directory by county, which is one reason Pennsylvania pages convert well into local next steps.

facts.label: Residential design flow
facts.note: Connecticut public guidance uses 150 gallons per bedroom per day for residential design sewage flow.

facts.label: Potential-bedroom risk
facts.value: Applies
facts.note: Connecticut is unusual because potential bedrooms and change-in-use risk can matter even when current occupancy is lower.

facts.label: Reserve area and additions
facts.value: Code-complying area must still work
facts.note: Building additions and changes in use are tied to code-complying area and reserve area concerns, which is why Connecticut estimates should stay conservative.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local director of health or approved agent
facts.sourceSection: Approval to construct and discharge procedures
facts.note: The local director of health or approved agent controls the practical next step once a homeowner moves beyond broad online sizing logic.

facts.label: First official step
facts.value: Site evaluation
facts.note: Oregon is one of the clearest states where site evaluation comes before permit certainty, which is why tank-size-only answers need extra caution.

facts.label: What site evaluation does not do
facts.value: Does not guarantee a specific system type
facts.note: Oregon DEQ says the site evaluation does not guarantee approval of a specific system type, so online sizing estimates must stay broader.

facts.label: Records lookup path
facts.value: Online septic system records available
facts.note: Oregon is strong for records-first workflow because DEQ provides an official path to locate septic system records online.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: County or local onsite authority
facts.note: After the estimate, Oregon homeowners usually need the local onsite authority directory more than a generic statewide article.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local agency issues OWTS permits
facts.note: California's OWTS Policy explicitly authorizes local agencies such as county environmental health departments to issue OWTS permits.

facts.label: Program structure
facts.value: Tier 1 default or Tier 2 LAMP
facts.note: California homeowners can land in the default Tier 1 path or a Regional Water Board-approved LAMP, which is why county-level routing matters so much.

facts.label: LAMP variation risk
facts.value: LAMP standards may differ from Tier 1
facts.note: California's approved LAMPs can use local siting and design standards that differ from the default Tier 1 program.

facts.label: Where to verify locally
facts.value: Regional board and local agency contact list
facts.note: California publishes a current OWTS regional-jurisdiction and local-agency contact list, which is the right first stop before trusting a quote.

facts.label: Permit requirement
facts.value: Permit and approved plan required
facts.note: Texas states plainly that a permit and approved plan are required before most OSSF construction, alteration, repair, extension, or operation.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local permitting authority
facts.note: Texas homeowners need the local permitting authority quickly because TCEQ says local programs can be more stringent than state minimums.

facts.label: Permit review window
facts.note: Texas says the permitting authority must approve or deny the permit application and planning materials within 30 days of receipt.

facts.label: First technical step
facts.value: Licensed site evaluator or PE
facts.note: Texas requires a site evaluation by a licensed site evaluator or professional engineer before the real system choice becomes clear.

facts.label: Local authority lookup
facts.value: OARS county contact search
facts.note: Texas OARS is useful for homeowners because TCEQ says it gives the general public contact information for local permitting authorities.

facts.label: Conventional pumping recommendation
facts.value: 3 to 5
facts.note: Texas recommends pumping conventional systems every three to five years, while also warning that some permitting authorities may impose stricter maintenance rules.

facts.label: Residential scope
facts.value: Less than 1,000 gallons per day
facts.note: New York's Appendix 75-A applies to residential onsite wastewater treatment systems serving properties under 1,000 gallons per day.

facts.label: Minimum daily flow
facts.note: New York's Appendix 75-A says new construction is based on a minimum daily flow of 110 gallons per bedroom.

facts.label: Specific waiver path
facts.value: State or designated local health official
facts.note: Appendix 75-A allows specific waivers to be issued by the State Commissioner of Health or a designated city county or part-county health department official.

facts.label: Primary local path
facts.value: District office or county health department
facts.sourceSection: Field offices and local health departments
facts.note: New York publishes a field-office and local-health-department directory because homeowners still need the right district office or county health department on the file.

facts.label: Program scope
facts.value: One-, two-, or three-family dwellings
facts.sourceSection: Who regulates household sewage treatment systems?
facts.note: Ohio EPA's homeowner FAQ says household sewage treatment systems serving one-, two-, or three-family dwellings are regulated under Chapter 3701-29 through the Ohio Department of Health.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local health departments
facts.sourceSection: Who regulates household sewage treatment systems?
facts.note: Ohio EPA says local health departments are responsible for permitting, code enforcement, nuisance investigations, and operational inspections for household sewage systems.

facts.label: Permit requirement
facts.value: Installation permit and operation permit
facts.note: Chapter 3701-29 includes installation-permit and operation-permit requirements for HSTS and SFOSTS work, which is why Ohio homeowners need the local permit file early.

facts.label: Off-lot discharge wrinkle
facts.value: NPDES coverage may apply
facts.note: Ohio EPA says household sewage treatment systems that discharge off lot may need NPDES general permit coverage, even though the local health department remains the first point of contact.

facts.label: Program structure
facts.value: Required service for local health departments
facts.note: EGLE describes onsite wastewater management as a required service for local health departments under the Michigan Public Health Code.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local health departments permit and inspect
facts.note: Michigan's environmental regulations guide says the onsite sanitary wastewater program is administered in coordination with local health departments that permit and inspect systems.

facts.label: If failure is suspected
facts.value: Contact local health department
facts.sourceSection: What should I do if I suspect my septic system has failed
facts.note: EGLE's public septic FAQ tells homeowners to contact the local health department if they suspect the system has failed.

facts.label: If system location is unclear
facts.value: Contact local health department
facts.sourceSection: I need to know where my septic system is located
facts.note: EGLE's public septic FAQ tells homeowners to contact the local health department if they are unsure where the septic system is located.

facts.label: Local variation risk
facts.value: Communities may add local ordinance requirements
facts.note: Michigan's environmental regulations guide notes that some communities may have local ordinance requirements in addition to the statewide framework.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: ADEQ delegated authority to all 15 counties
facts.note: ADEQ says it has delegated permitting authority for onsite wastewater treatment facilities to all 15 Arizona counties.

facts.label: Where to apply
facts.value: Submit permit application to the county
facts.note: ADEQ's homeowner page says applicants submit the permit application to the county in which the property is located.

facts.label: Type 4 permit scope
facts.value: 0 to 24,000 gallons per day
facts.note: ADEQ's Type 4 general permit page says the permit authorizes onsite wastewater treatment facilities from 0 to 24,000 gallons per day.

facts.label: Permit sequence
facts.value: Notice of Intent to Construct, then Notice of Intent to Discharge
facts.note: ADEQ describes a Notice of Intent to Construct, a two-year window to construct, and a Notice of Intent to Discharge before the permit stays valid for the life of the facility.

facts.label: First technical document
facts.value: Uniform Site Investigation Report
facts.note: ADEQ's delegation page points homeowners to delegated county forms and the Uniform Site Investigation Report used by those county programs.

facts.label: Transfer and ownership lookup
facts.value: Search prior notices of transfer
facts.note: ADEQ's OWN portal lets users search prior notices of transfer for onsite wastewater treatment facilities.

facts.label: Permit trigger
facts.value: Permit required before install alter or repair
facts.note: CDPHE's OWTS page says a permit is required before installing, altering, or repairing an onsite wastewater treatment system.

facts.label: Primary authority
facts.value: Local public health agencies usually regulate up to 2,000 gpd
facts.note: CDPHE says local public health agencies typically implement and regulate onsite wastewater systems with design capacities of 2,000 gallons per day or less.

facts.label: First technical document
facts.value: Site and Soil Evaluation Report may be required
facts.note: CDPHE's homeowner-facing OWTS page notes that the local public health agency may require the Site and Soil Evaluation Report form.

facts.label: Records and buyer context
facts.value: Permit data and transfer-of-title resources are local-path driven
facts.note: CDPHE's OWTS page points homeowners to Colorado county OWTS permit data and a map of local agencies that implement transfer of title inspections.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: Use the local public health agency directory
facts.note: CDPHE's public directory is the cleanest statewide way to identify the local public health agency that owns the OWTS file.

facts.label: Construction permit trigger
facts.value: Construction permit from the health department or VDH
facts.note: VDH's onsite sewage page says owners must obtain a construction permit from the health department or VDH before a sewage disposal system is installed.

facts.label: Systems with ongoing permits
facts.value: AOSS and private pumped systems can carry operation permits
facts.note: VDH's onsite sewage page notes that some systems are eligible for operation permits, including alternative onsite sewage systems and private pumped systems.

facts.label: Conventional maintenance cadence
facts.value: Pump out every 3 to 5 years
facts.note: VDH's homeowner handbook says a conventional onsite sewage system generally should be pumped out every 3 to 5 years.

facts.label: AOSS inspection cadence
facts.value: Some AOSS need operator visits every 3 6 or 12 months
facts.note: VDH's homeowner handbook says some AOSS require a licensed operator to visit every 3 months and others every 6 or 12 months depending on the permit and system.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: Use the local health district directory
facts.note: VDH's local health districts page is the cleanest statewide way to identify the district and environmental health office that handles the parcel.

facts.label: Records to request
facts.value: Permit operation permit O&M manual inspection reports as-built drawings notice of recordation
facts.note: VDH's buyer guidance tells homeowners to ask for permits, operation permits, the operation and maintenance manual, inspection reports, as-built drawings, and any notice of recordation tied to the system.

facts.label: Permit scope
facts.value: Construction permits cover installation and repair work
facts.note: TDEC's SSDS permit hub says septic construction permits cover installation and repair work.

facts.label: When to get the permit
facts.value: Obtain permit before dirt work or building pad
facts.note: TDEC's online septic services page says a septic permit should always be obtained before starting dirt work or before a building pad is poured over the septic system area.

facts.label: Failing-system permit
facts.value: Repair permit required before work on a failing system
facts.note: TDEC's online septic services page says a repair permit is required before work begins on a failing septic system.

facts.label: Transaction record
facts.value: Inspection Letter for sale mortgage or subdivision review
facts.note: TDEC's online septic services page says inspection letters document the status of an existing septic system and are routinely requested for sales, mortgages, and subdivision approval.

facts.label: When the system widens
facts.value: Alternative systems when soil or site conditions are not favorable
facts.note: TDEC's online septic services page says alternative septic systems are used in cases where soil and site conditions are not favorable for a conventional system.

facts.label: Who owns the first call
facts.value: Contract counties have their own septic assistance services
facts.note: TDEC's contacts page says counties listed as contract counties have their own septic system assistance services.

facts.label: Program admin
facts.value: Onsite sewage program coordinated in county health departments
facts.note: ADPH says the Soil and Onsite Sewage Branch coordinates the onsite sewage program in county health departments.

facts.label: Permit to install
facts.value: Application for Permit to Install required before construction
facts.note: ADPH says homeowners must submit an Application for a Permit to Install to the local county health department before construction begins.

facts.label: Soil-test trigger
facts.value: Soil testing or percolation test may be required
facts.sourceSection: Can I Live On This Lot
facts.note: ADPH says that when sewer is unavailable, homeowners may need soil testing or a percolation test before installing an onsite sewage system.

facts.label: Permit-file retrieval
facts.value: Owners agents use local health department and non-owners use records request for permit copies
facts.note: ADPH says property owners or their agents can request septic tank information from the local health department, while non-owners can use the Records Request path for a permit copy.

facts.label: Completed permit file
facts.value: Approval for Use includes system diagram
facts.note: ADPH says the completed permit, also called the Approval for Use, includes a diagram of the actual system installation and other system information.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: County health department locations and contacts published statewide
facts.note: ADPH publishes a statewide directory of county health departments and public health districts so homeowners can identify the local office handling onsite sewage questions.

facts.label: Program scope
facts.value: IDPH regulates private sewage disposal systems
facts.note: IDPH says Illinois regulates private sewage disposal systems and provides homeowner-facing guidance from the statewide program page.

facts.label: Local review path
facts.value: About 90 local health departments review construction plans
facts.note: IDPH says about 90 local health departments review private sewage disposal construction plans either under local ordinance or as state agents.

facts.label: Code set
facts.value: Private Sewage Disposal Code plus related licensing and mound rules
facts.note: IDPH's laws-and-rules page links the Private Sewage Disposal Licensing Act, Private Sewage Disposal Code, and Private Sewage Mound Code.

facts.label: Evaluation-form risk
facts.value: Flagged conditions may require repair or replacement
facts.sourceSection: Private Sewage Disposal System Evaluation Form
facts.note: The Illinois evaluation-form PDF warns that flagged conditions can mean the sewage system needs repair or replacement.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: Regional and local health department directory published statewide
facts.note: IDPH publishes a directory and map of regional and local health departments so homeowners can identify the practical office holding the file.

facts.label: Program structure
facts.value: On-Site Systems Division supports county health departments and local approving authorities
facts.note: MDE says the On-Site Systems Division gives technical assistance to county health departments and local approving authorities.

facts.label: Who owns the file
facts.value: County local approving authority directory published statewide
facts.note: MDE publishes a statewide local approving authority directory for onsite-system questions and files.

facts.label: PTI workflow
facts.value: Property Transfer Inspector licensing applies
facts.note: MDE's PTI page describes the property-transfer inspector licensing path used in the current Maryland transfer workflow.

facts.label: Transfer file search
facts.value: Proper PTI includes file search homeowner interview inspection and report
facts.note: MDE's property-transfer guidance says a proper PTI includes a file search, homeowner interview, inspection or evaluation, and a report.

facts.label: What the file can reveal
facts.value: Age type design location soils perc complaints and violations
facts.note: MDE's guidance says the file search may surface age, type, design, location, soils or perc information, complaints, and violations.

facts.label: File-access friction
facts.value: File search may require a Public Information Act request
facts.note: MDE's property-transfer guidance says retrieving the local file may require a Public Information Act request in some cases.

facts.label: Inspection admin
facts.value: Counties have primary responsibility to inspect POWTS
facts.note: DSPS says counties have primary responsibility to inspect private onsite wastewater treatment systems.

facts.label: Permit path
facts.value: Sanitary permit application goes to the county
facts.note: DSPS says sanitary permit applications for POWTS are submitted to the county.

facts.label: Review structure
facts.value: Plan review may route through delegated counties or the department
facts.note: DSPS says plan review may be completed by a delegated county or by the department depending on the project.

facts.label: Maintenance program
facts.value: All POWTS entered maintenance tracking on October 1 2019
facts.note: DSPS says all POWTS entered the maintenance tracking program on October 1 2019.

facts.label: Inspection interval
facts.value: Visual inspection at least once every three years
facts.note: DSPS says POWTS are visually inspected at least once every three years under the maintenance program.

facts.label: Inspection form
facts.value: POWTS inspection report form used in the file path
facts.note: DSPS publishes the POWTS inspection-report form used in the inspection and permit workflow.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: Delegated-agent county contact list published statewide
facts.note: DSPS publishes a delegated-agent contact list so homeowners can identify the county or local office that handles POWTS review and files.

facts.label: Program admin
facts.value: Parish sanitarians in local health units operate permitting workflow
facts.note: LDH's wastewater program says parish sanitarians in local health units inform, advise, and operate the onsite wastewater permitting path.

facts.label: Permit packet path
facts.value: Permit application information published statewide
facts.note: LDH publishes permit-application information for onsite wastewater approvals and application routing.

facts.label: Sewer-availability gate
facts.value: Community sewer must be used when available
facts.note: LDH's septic-tank application packet says community sewer must be used when available.

facts.label: Who applies
facts.value: Homeowner must be the applicant
facts.note: LDH's application packet says the homeowner must be the applicant for the onsite wastewater permit path.

facts.label: Application input
facts.value: Legible property plat required
facts.note: LDH's application packet says a legible property plat is required before the permit file is complete.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: Parish and local-office directory published statewide
facts.note: LDH publishes a directory that routes homeowners to the parish or local health unit handling onsite wastewater questions.

facts.label: Sewer-availability gate
facts.value: Onsite systems prohibited where sanitary sewer is available within a reasonable distance
facts.note: IDOH's onsite sewage systems page says onsite sewage systems are prohibited where sanitary sewer is available within a reasonable distance and connection is required.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: County environmental territory contacts published statewide
facts.note: IDOH publishes county environmental territory contacts so homeowners can identify the local office handling onsite sewage questions.

facts.label: Program admin
facts.value: Residential onsite sewage program administered by local boards of health
facts.note: Indiana's 410 IAC 6-8.3 says the residential onsite sewage systems program is administered by local boards of health.

facts.label: Residential design flow
facts.value: 150 gallons per day per bedroom
facts.note: Indiana's 410 IAC 6-8.3 uses 150 gallons per day per bedroom for residential sewage design flow.

facts.label: Local variation
facts.value: Local ordinances may be more stringent or interpreted differently
facts.note: IDOH's technical-review page says local health departments may have more stringent ordinances or may vary in interpretation from the state minimums.

facts.label: Program admin
facts.value: ECLS administers onsite sewage treatment through 21 local offices
facts.note: DEQ says its Environmental Complaints and Local Services division administers Oklahoma's onsite sewage treatment program through 21 local offices.

facts.label: First real step
facts.value: Soil test
facts.note: DEQ's homeowner page says the first step in determining the type of onsite sewage system needed is to get a soil test.

facts.label: What drives the design
facts.value: Soil results property size and number of bedrooms
facts.note: DEQ says the system is designed and approved based on soil-test results, property size, and number of bedrooms.

facts.label: Request path
facts.value: Soil tests permits to construct and existing-system evaluations
facts.note: DEQ's request-for-service page lists soil tests, authorizations or permits to construct, and existing-system evaluations as homeowner request options.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: Contact DEQ Environmental Specialist by county
facts.note: DEQ's FAQ tells homeowners to contact the DEQ Environmental Specialist by county for soil-test and onsite sewage questions.

facts.label: When perc is not enough
facts.value: Soil profiles used for all approved systems and perc tests fit only some conventional paths
facts.note: DEQ's soil-profilers page says soil profiles are used for all approved systems while perc tests fit only certain conventional, lagoon, or aerobic spray paths.

facts.label: Program admin
facts.value: Onsite sewage program administered through local health departments
facts.note: Kentucky's program page says the onsite sewage disposal systems program is administered through local health departments.

facts.label: First real step
facts.value: Onsite evaluation of site and soil suitability
facts.note: Kentucky says the onsite sewage disposal systems program begins with onsite evaluations to determine site and soil suitability.

facts.label: Who performs inspections
facts.value: Local health department inspectors perform site evaluations and inspections
facts.note: Kentucky's program page says local health department inspectors perform site evaluations and inspections.

facts.label: Permit packet path
facts.value: Site-evaluation application and OSDS construction permit go through the local health department
facts.note: Kentucky's homeowner brochure says the site-evaluation application and OSDS construction permit are submitted through the local health department.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: Local health department directory published statewide
facts.note: Kentucky publishes a statewide local health department listing so homeowners can identify the office holding the onsite sewage file.

facts.label: How common septic is
facts.value: More than 600000 homes and businesses use septic systems
facts.note: MPCA says more than 600,000 homes and businesses use septic systems in Minnesota.

facts.label: Program admin
facts.value: State rules implemented and enforced through local ordinances
facts.note: MPCA says statewide SSTS rules are implemented and enforced through local ordinances.

facts.label: Local program footprint
facts.value: About 200 local SSTS programs review permits and inspect systems
facts.note: MPCA says about 200 local SSTS programs review permits and inspect new and replacement systems.

facts.label: Transfer wrinkle
facts.value: Some local governments require compliance inspections before property transfer
facts.note: MPCA says some local governments require compliance inspections prior to property transfer.

facts.label: Disclosure is not inspection
facts.value: No statewide compliance inspection required before sale but disclosure is not the same as an inspection
facts.note: MPCA's disclosure PDF says no statewide compliance inspection is required before sale, but disclosure is not the same as a compliance inspection and many local ordinances or lenders still require one.

facts.label: Prior report disclosure
facts.value: Seller must attach prior inspection report in seller possession
facts.note: Minnesota statute 115.55 requires the seller to attach any prior septic inspection report in the seller's possession to the disclosure.

facts.label: Permit requirement
facts.value: Site approvals and permits required for all septic systems
facts.note: SCDES says South Carolina law requires site approvals and permits for all septic systems.

facts.label: Building-permit gate
facts.value: Septic permit before county building permit
facts.note: SCDES says the septic permit must be issued before the county can issue a building permit for the structure.

facts.label: Permit sequence
facts.value: D-1740 then site visit then Permit to Construct and final inspection
facts.note: SCDES describes filing the D-1740 with a plat or deed, the agency site visit, a Permit to Construct for a traditional system, and a final inspection before the permit path is complete.

facts.label: Permit life
facts.value: Permit valid for five years if construction has not started
facts.note: SCDES says the permit is good for five years if construction has not started.

facts.label: Permit-file retrieval
facts.value: Permit copy lookup by parcel details
facts.note: SCDES says homeowners can request permit copies on file and that parcel details help locate the record.

facts.label: Who to call first
facts.value: County-specific contact for permit copies final inspections and after-submission questions
facts.note: SCDES routes permit copies, final inspections, and after-submission questions through county-specific contacts on its septic tanks contact page.

facts.label: Primary rule context
facts.value: Title 5
facts.note: Massachusetts tank-size and septic planning questions are usually entangled with Title 5, not just a generic statewide gallon table.

facts.label: Buyer and seller timing
facts.value: Inspection and transfer timing can change urgency
facts.sourceSection: Buying or selling property with a septic system
facts.note: Massachusetts homeowners often discover septic sizing and replacement questions because a sale or transfer forces the issue through Title 5 timing.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local Board of Health
facts.note: Massachusetts owners usually need the local Board of Health path once they move from a planning estimate into a real upgrade or repair conversation.

facts.label: Primary workflow split
facts.value: DEP-managed counties versus county health path
facts.sourceSection: Statewide onsite sewage and septic overview
facts.note: Florida's first homeowner question is often jurisdiction, not tank size, because the practical path can run through DEP-managed counties or county health departments.

facts.label: Primary permit step
facts.value: Construction approvals matter before trusting the low end
facts.note: Florida's public permit path makes construction approvals visible, which is why a simple tank-size answer can be less useful than jurisdiction and permit context.

facts.label: Buyer-side records risk
facts.value: Homebuyer guide and permit trail matter
facts.note: Florida buyer questions often hinge on records, operating-permit history, and who currently owns the regulatory path.

facts.label: Gravity system cadence
facts.value: At least every 3 years
facts.note: Washington is unusually clear that gravity systems are inspected at least every three years, which makes maintenance context more concrete than in many states.

facts.label: Advanced system cadence
facts.value: At least every year
facts.note: Washington gives homeowners a concrete split between gravity and advanced systems, which is why inspection, O&M logs, and system type belong in the estimate conversation.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local health jurisdiction
facts.sourceSection: Management roles for onsite sewage systems
facts.note: Washington homeowners usually need the local health jurisdiction before they need a more elaborate online estimate.

facts.label: Local authority lookup
facts.value: Official local health jurisdiction directory
facts.note: Washington has a strong local lookup path, which supports records-first and permit-first workflow pages.

facts.label: Primary rule context
facts.value: Management program and local oversight risk
facts.note: New Jersey pages work because the state can feel more like an ongoing management and reporting problem than a simple tank-size question.

facts.label: Special-area risk
facts.value: Pinelands certification and maintenance obligations
facts.note: Pinelands context is one reason New Jersey needs state-specific pages instead of a generic national calculator pitch.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local board of health or local health department
facts.note: New Jersey homeowners usually need local health review and records next, especially where management ordinances or Pinelands rules apply.

facts.label: Primary permit structure
facts.value: Improvement permit, construction authorization, operation permit
facts.note: North Carolina's permit ladder is one of its strongest differentiators, and it matters more to homeowners than a one-number tank answer.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: County health department
facts.note: North Carolina usually turns into a county health department workflow quickly, so local office context belongs next to any sizing estimate.

facts.label: Program context
facts.value: On-site water protection branch oversight
facts.note: North Carolina homeowner pages are strongest when they explain the permit ladder and county path together.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: County or local public health authority
facts.note: Missouri is strong for local workflow content because the path can run through different county or local authorities.

facts.label: County-by-county routing
facts.value: Jurisdiction differs by county
facts.sourceSection: Onsite sewage permitting jurisdiction by county
facts.note: Missouri's county-by-county authority map is one reason local verification belongs very early in the homeowner workflow.

facts.label: Local public health lookup
facts.value: Official local public health agency directory
facts.note: Missouri homeowners often need the local public health agency before they need more theoretical estimate detail.
